nodes.name: Sticky Note3

parameters.content:
## 3. Provide AI Service
Our service uses an LLM (OpenAI GPT4o-mini in this example) to organise the uploaded PDF's data into a structured JSON format. This conversion is useful for following integrations or data storage. In this example however, we'll use display it back to the user.

nodes.notes: This stickyNote node performs automated tasks as part of the workflow.

nodes.name: Sticky Note4

parameters.content:
## 4. Use Custom LLM Subnode to Track Usage & Cost
[Learn more about the Langchain Code Node]({{ $env.WEBHOOK_URL }}

By creating our custom LLM subnode using the Langchain Code node, we are able to tap into the chat completion's response which contains the token usage metadata for the information extractor request.

With this, we can calculate exactly how much the client's request is costing per use!

The only remaining step then is to store
this data somewhere. Rather than importing
more dependencies or hardcoding more
credentials, a novel solution can be to attach
a tool to our subnode.

Here, we've decided to use the Google Sheets
tool and append the client's usage metrics to
the sheet. Check it out here - [Client Usage Log](
{{ $env.WEBHOOK_URL }}

nodes.name: Sticky Note5

parameters.content:
## 5. Automatically Send Invoice at End of Month (Optional)
With our client usage log, it's fairly simple to aggregate the log records for the client within a certain timeframe and calculate the totals.
This can satisfy accurate billing requirements from clients or at least, allows your team to understand how much each client is costing and budget accordingly.

nodes.notes: This stickyNote node performs automated tasks as part of the workflow.

nodes.name: Sticky Note6

parameters.content:
## Try It Out!
### This n8n template demonstrates how to use the Langchain code node to track token usage and cost for every LLM call.

This is useful if your templates handle multiple clients or customers and you need a cheap and easy way to capture how much of your AI credits they are using.

### How it works
* In our mock AI service, we're offering a data conversion API to convert Resume PDFs into JSON documents.
* A form trigger is used to allow for PDF upload and the file is parsed using the Extract from File node.
* An Edit Fields node is used to capture additional variables to send to our log.
* Next, we use the Information Extractor node to organise the Resume data into the given JSON schema.
* The LLM subnode attached to the Information Extractor is a custom one we've built using the Langchain Code node.
* With our custom LLM subnode, we're able to capture the usage metadata using lifecycle hooks.
* We've also attached a Google Sheet tool to our LLM subnode, allowing us to send our usage metadata to a google sheet.
* Finally, we demonstrate how you can aggregate from the google sheet to understand how much AI tokens/costs your clients are liable for.


**Check out the example Client Usage Log** - {{ $env.WEBHOOK_URL }}

### How to use
* **SELF-HOSTED N8N ONLY** - the Langchain Code node is only available in the self-hosted version of n8n. It is not available in n8n cloud.
* The LLM subnode can only be attached to non-"AI agent" nodes; Basic LLM node, Information Extractor, Question & Answer Chain, Sentiment Analysis, Summarization Chain and Text Classifier.

### Need Help?
Join the [Discord]({{ $env.WEBHOOK_URL }} or ask in the [Forum]({{ $env.WEBHOOK_URL }}

parameters.content:
### Update Workbook
This is the workbook which will track the token usage and costs.

## 1. Offer AI Service to Clients
Here, we'll using an n8n form to offer a document extraction service for Resume/CV PDFs. The user simply uploads a PDF and we return it in JSON format. This is just a simple example for demonstration purposes.

## 2. Gather External Variables to Send to Log
There are some variables we can't define in the subnode but we can add them here.

## 3. Provide AI Service
Our service uses an LLM (OpenAI GPT4o-mini in this example) to organise the uploaded PDF's data into a structured JSON format. This conversion is useful for following integrations or data storage. In this example however, we'll use display it back to the user.

## 4. Use Custom LLM Subnode to Track Usage & Cost
[Learn more about the Langchain Code Node]({{ $env.WEBHOOK_URL }}

By creating our custom LLM subnode using the Langchain Code node, we are able to tap into the chat completion's response which contains the token usage metadata for the information extractor request.

With this, we can calculate exactly how much the client's request is costing per use!

The only remaining step then is to store
this data somewhere. Rather than importing
more dependencies or hardcoding more
credentials, a novel solution can be to attach
a tool to our subnode.

Here, we've decided to use the Google Sheets
tool and append the client's usage metrics to
the sheet. Check it out here - [Client Usage Log](
{{ $env.WEBHOOK_URL }}

## 5. Automatically Send Invoice at End of Month (Optional)
With our client usage log, it's fairly simple to aggregate the log records for the client within a certain timeframe and calculate the totals.
This can satisfy accurate billing requirements from clients or at least, allows your team to understand how much each client is costing and budget accordingly.

## Try It Out!
### This n8n template demonstrates how to use the Langchain code node to track token usage and cost for every LLM call.

This is useful if your templates handle multiple clients or customers and you need a cheap and easy way to capture how much of your AI credits they are using.

### How it works
* In our mock AI service, we're offering a data conversion API to convert Resume PDFs into JSON documents.
* A form trigger is used to allow for PDF upload and the file is parsed using the Extract from File node.
* An Edit Fields node is used to capture additional variables to send to our log.
* Next, we use the Information Extractor node to organise the Resume data into the given JSON schema.
* The LLM subnode attached to the Information Extractor is a custom one we've built using the Langchain Code node.
* With our custom LLM subnode, we're able to capture the usage metadata using lifecycle hooks.
* We've also attached a Google Sheet tool to our LLM subnode, allowing us to send our usage metadata to a google sheet.
* Finally, we demonstrate how you can aggregate from the google sheet to understand how much AI tokens/costs your clients are liable for.


**Check out the example Client Usage Log** - {{ $env.WEBHOOK_URL }}

### How to use
* **SELF-HOSTED N8N ONLY** - the Langchain Code node is only available in the self-hosted version of n8n. It is not available in n8n cloud.
* The LLM subnode can only be attached to non-"AI agent" nodes; Basic LLM node, Information Extractor, Question & Answer Chain, Sentiment Analysis, Summarization Chain and Text Classifier.

### Need Help?
Join the [Discord]({{ $env.WEBHOOK_URL }} or ask in the [Forum]({{ $env.WEBHOOK_URL }}

### SELF-HOSTED N8N ONLY
Please note, this template only works in the self-hosted version of n8n only. It will not work in the cloud version.

prerequisites: An active n8n instance installed locally, in a Docker container, or hosted on n8n Cloud.
Basic familiarity with importing JSON templates in n8n.
setupInstructions: Download the JSON configuration for this workflow.
Ensure you set up credentials for all connected integrations.
Test your trigger and run execution.
requiredCredentials: google Sheets  O Auth2  A P I details
configurationSteps: Verify the settings of the trigger node "On form submission".
Open the scheduler node "Every End of Month".
Adjust the execution intervals (e.g., Every hour, Daily, or Custom CRON expression) according to your preferences.
Open the "Client Usage Log" Google Sheets node.
Ensure you select the spreadsheet file and specific tab/sheet from the dropdown menus.
Map the input JSON keys to your target sheet columns.
Open the "Custom LLM Subnode" node (n8n-nodes-base.noOp) and configure the action parameters, mappings, and fields.
Open the "Sticky Note" node (n8n-nodes-base.stickyNote) and configure the action parameters, mappings, and fields.
Open the "Sticky Note1" node (n8n-nodes-base.stickyNote) and configure the action parameters, mappings, and fields.
Open the "Sticky Note2" node (n8n-nodes-base.stickyNote) and configure the action parameters, mappings, and fields.
Open the "Sticky Note3" node (n8n-nodes-base.stickyNote) and configure the action parameters, mappings, and fields.
Open the "Sticky Note4" node (n8n-nodes-base.stickyNote) and configure the action parameters, mappings, and fields.
Open the "Display JSON Document" node (n8n-nodes-base.form) and configure the action parameters, mappings, and fields.
Open the "Filter Last Month" node (n8n-nodes-base.filter) and configure the action parameters, mappings, and fields.
Open the "Get Client Logs" Google Sheets node.
Ensure you select the spreadsheet file and specific tab/sheet from the dropdown menus.
Map the input JSON keys to your target sheet columns.
Open the "Calculate Totals" node (n8n-nodes-base.summarize) and configure the action parameters, mappings, and fields.
Open the "Send Invoice" node (n8n-nodes-base.gmail) and configure the action parameters, mappings, and fields.
Open the "Sticky Note5" node (n8n-nodes-base.stickyNote) and configure the action parameters, mappings, and fields.
Open the "Sticky Note6" node (n8n-nodes-base.stickyNote) and configure the action parameters, mappings, and fields.
Open the "Sticky Note7" node (n8n-nodes-base.stickyNote) and configure the action parameters, mappings, and fields.
Open the "Parse PDF Upload" node (n8n-nodes-base.extractFromFile) and configure the action parameters, mappings, and fields.
Open the "Extract Resume Data" node (n8n-nodes-base.noOp) and configure the action parameters, mappings, and fields.
Open the "Error Handler" node (n8n-nodes-base.stopAndError) and configure the action parameters, mappings, and fields.
Locate the Set/Edit fields node "Logging Attributes".
Define or edit values for variables that are referenced in downstream nodes.
testingSteps: Click the "Test step" or "Listen for test event" button on your Trigger node.
Send a test trigger from your source service (e.g. fill a test form or trigger a webhook trigger).
Verify that the node canvas lights up green, indicating successful data flow.
Open the final destination node to confirm that output records were saved correctly.
instructions.expectedResult: Incoming triggers will qualified/formatted, new rows will be written to Google Sheets, and email replies or alerts will be sent automatically.
troubleshooting: Check credential warning symbols. Most workflow issues stem from expired OAuth connections or missing API permissions.
Verify that the data keys coming from your Trigger node match the exact names mapped in subsequent nodes (e.g., {{ $json.body.email }}).
Open the Execution history tab in n8n to view logs and determine which node failed.
securityNotes: Never hardcode API keys, passwords, or authentication bearer tokens in the plain text fields of node parameters.
If you download or share this workflow JSON, sanitize any sensitive parameters or configuration nodes.
customizationIdeas: Add an email node at the end to notify you of successful executions.
Incorporate an AI LLM node to summarize trigger payloads before writing them to integrations.
